summary: In response to a major data industry merger, Reis offers pragmatic advice for practitioners navigating the volatile landscape of data tools and vendors. He argues that VC-funded tools inevitably face consolidation pressure, and practitioners should avoid over-reliance on any single technology while being more grateful contributors to open source.
key_points: VC investment in data tools comes with strings attached, requiring massive exits through M&A or IPO
Practitioners should always have a Plan B and budget for potential cost changes when relying on funded tools
Open source users often act entitled without contributing back to projects or communities
AI coding tools will enable teams to build custom solutions by cherry-picking functionality rather than adopting entire projects
Tool popularity and 'coolness' are temporary - everything eventually becomes legacy
money_quotes: The Golden Rule is the person who owns the gold makes the rules. And investment money comes with strings attached, namely hit homeruns and make massive exits.
Tools are like goldfish. They're great while they're around, but they come and go quickly. Don't tie your whole identity or job to one specific technology or tool.

Quite a few people using open-source are ungrateful, entitled, and cheap AF. It amazes me when I see people asking the world from open-source projects, but also aren't contributing to these projects or their community.
With AI coding tools getting better every week, we'll likely see teams developing custom-built solutions for specific workflows. Just cherry pick the parts you want and use AI to create it.
key_insight: In an industry driven by VC exit pressure and constant consolidation, data practitioners must stay flexible, avoid tool-based identity, and prepare for change as the only constant.
